LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Sign in Join now

Declining job applications: My process and results

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Greg Wyatt
8mo Edited
  • Report this post

Here's my process for declining job applications, with an example from last week. On Friday, I spent 1hr40 declining around 220 applications for one vacancy. An advert which I had closed the previous day, after 72 hours, mainly because of the volume of qualified applications. Normally I'd leave this for at least one week, but there's only one vacancy, and many of these applicants are between roles. I'd previously reviewed their applications, reading CVs and other available information such as LinkedIn profiles, twice (the second as an error check), establishing they weren't suitable for the vacancy context. This was part of a multichannel approach including sourcing, with the final shortlist of assessed candidates coming from an even split. I spoke with around 30 candidates in total. 1hr30 was on 100 applications on LinkedIn, using their terrible dashboard, (see comments for an experiment I've run with this). It's clunkiness was why this was so slow. 10 minutes on c120 emailed applications, because a response is simple to automate. I used a template message for all: - thanks for your interest - have moved forwards with candidates that meet these criteria (driving fit for purpose structure in a scaling environment) - offer of a free copy of A Career Breakdown Kit, my 5* rated book on navigating our VUCA marketplace Around 60% replied to say thank you. 1 was snarky. 2 disagreed, so I gave them more specific feedback. Of the respondents, 30 have asked for the book, which I hope proves helpful. It's paid for by my reciprocate campaign, notionally at £7 a copy (which would be my royalty on a book sale). I find that second point above is proving effective, given it provides a specific reason which their application lacked, and allows a response at scale that has meaning. I adjust it based on the specifics of the vacancy. My principle is to reciprocate the level of investment an applicant has made, which is a fair balance considering the volume of applications every advert experiences. So if we've spoken or if they progress in the process - I'll support them appropriately, including free coaching on improving their approach (again paid by my Reciprocate campaign). -- That time investment would be lower were I not using the LinkedIn UI as part an separate experiment into their AI filters. With light automation, I'd bring it down significantly. There's no reason every employer can't do something similar, or at least give a response, especially with automation. -- I've kept this pretty top level - happy to answer any questions.

52 25 Comments
Like Comment
Jessica Rietbrock 8mo
  • Report this comment

It's really nice to hear of a recruiter taking the time to follow up with specific insight - you're going above and beyond with additional resources!! I agree that there is no excuse for bad, generic, copy and pasted feedback, or worse still, no feedback/ghosting. I once had an internal TA not show up for a first round interview, and ONLY email me to give an explanation when after 30 mins of sitting in an online meeting alone, I decided to ask if the call was still going ahead. That has entirely shattered my trust in this company (which apparently was a dodged bullet anyway ha ha). I don't think enough thought is put into the way it damages the reputation of the organisation as a whole when the Hiring Manager/TA/Recruiter doesn't handle the process properly!

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Karl Simpson 8mo
  • Report this comment

Love the fact that you embrace the reciprocity concept. As you rightly mention (good) candidates invest time in personalising their job applications to the role and although a “no” can be disheartening even a small amount of useful feedback can bring closure and value in order for them to move forward with their search. For the recruiter of course having efficient systems is key!

More Relevant Posts

  • khaldoun eid
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    MindsLeb 🚀 STOP Applying. START Marketing. Your CV Isn't a History Book, It's a Sales Pitch. After 8 years as a Team Manager and Hiring Manager, I've reviewed thousands of applications. I can tell you the honest truth: most CVs get ignored not because the candidates lack skill, but because they lack strategic marketing. To secure the interview, you need a fundamental shift in mindset: Move from Historian to Marketer. 🔑 The Core Shift: Prove Value, Don't List Duties As a hiring manager, I am looking for a solution, not a history lesson. Your application documents must answer one critical question immediately: "How will this person solve my specific problems and deliver measurable results?" The Ineffective "Historian" Mindset: Focuses on the past, using passive language to describe what you were responsible for (e.g., "Responsible for managing team projects"). The Effective "Marketer" Mindset: Focuses on the future, using strong action verbs to highlight the value delivered and the problems solved (e.g., "Reduced project overruns by 15% by implementing a new resource tracking system"). The Goal: Stop reporting history and start selling solutions. 💡 Strategy 1: The Job Description is Your Cheat Sheet From my perspective as a hiring manager, the Job Description (JD) is my internal checklist. If your application doesn't align with it, it gets passed over. Deconstruct: Identify the Top 4-5 Critical Requirements (the non-negotiables) in the JD. These are the keywords I am searching for. Translate: Use the JD’s exact keywords to rewrite your Professional Summary and Experience sections. This is the only way to beat the Applicant Tracking System (ATS) and grab human attention. Quantify Everything: Show the impact. Use numbers, percentages, and currencies to demonstrate success. Example transformation: Instead of, "Oversaw data quality checks," use: "Improved data integrity scores by 25% by implementing new validation protocols." ✉️ Strategy 2: The Cover Letter is the Direct Alignment Proof The cover letter (or application email) is the Executive Summary you write for me, the manager. It must be precise and targeted—I don't have time for generic paragraphs. The Opening Hook: Start with a high-impact statement showing your unique fit for the role. Direct Alignment Matrix: Use the body to clearly address the core JD requirements with specific, evidence-based examples from your career. Attractiveness comes from relevance and proof. Show, Don't Tell: For every requirement, provide a concise, evidence-based example of how you've handled it before. If the JD asks for: "Proven ability to lead complex process improvement projects." You state: "I have four years of hands-on experience leading cross-functional process redesigns, including a project that streamlined our order-to-cash process, cutting lead time by 20%." #JobSearchTips #CVWriting #CareerGrowth #InterviewTips #HiringManagerSecrets #ProfessionalDevelopment #lebanon MindsLeb

    1 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Ryan Yoneyama
    7mo
    • Report this post

    There has been a lot of talk about ATS systems and how AI plays a role, and it leaves candidates wondering what they are supposed to do to actually get their resume seen by recruiters and the hiring team. The primary question I've seen asked is: what do I have to do to my resume to optimize it for the ATS or the recruiter? As a tenured recruiter, I'll tell you and shed some light on what actually matters in a resume. Myth 1: All ATS are run by AI and they prioritize resumes with only the right keywords. Reality: No, this is not accurate. An ATS is like a recruiter's CRM where all their leads are hosted (sales folks, you'll understand), and all the ATS allows recruiters to do is get a clear picture of their pipeline and the metrics associated with that pipeline. When you apply to a position, your information is populated into the ATS, and the recruiter can now view your resume and move you through the pipeline or disposition. It doesn't prioritize resumes or profiles; it shows us your profile according to when you apply, that's it. Myth 2: My resume wasn't good enough according to the AI; that's why I was auto-rejected at 12am. Reality: That's not how ATS works. A real person (the recruiter or hiring manager) has to go in and take action on a profile. As AI becomes better, I have seen some tools that provide a score of how strong a resume is against the requirements a job description lists, but even if your resume scored a 30%, the recruiter still would have to manually action the rejection. The scoring system also purely scores off of keywords too - you could have only a few keywords but still have incredible results, and the tool scores your resume as a 30%, but then you get hired. For the weird times you might be getting rejected? Some people are always working, even in the middle of the night. I've known a few people that are, in fact, working at 2:30am, but that's another conversation in itself. Myth 3: You have to customize your resume to the job description. Reality: This will be a separate post this week, but short answer is no, not for every job. Will touch on this later, but unless you're entirely pivoting careers, one resume is usually fine to use to apply to multiple roles. Just remember, very rarely is there one reason as to why something isn't working. Oh, and while AI is being used to increase repetitive activities for recruiters, it's just that - an aid. Your favorite recruiters like me are still around 😎. If you like content like this follow Ryan Yoneyama for more 🤙

    2
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Chelsea-Jane Merryweather
    7mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Lately I’ve noticed a wave of posts giving advice to job seekers, and honestly, some of it makes me cringe. “Match every keyword exactly.” “Rebuild your résumé for each application.” “Use AI to make your résumé more ‘ATS friendly.’” Let’s be honest, that’s not good advice. It shifts the responsibility to candidates to make themselves algorithm-friendly instead of encouraging recruiters to do their actual job: reading between the lines, spotting potential, and making human decisions. AI should assist recruiting, not replace the human part of it. And good recruiters, yes, they’re still out there, still take the time to look and to understand a person’s experience. But here’s the reality: when your résumé lands in front of a human, you have seconds to make an impression. So make those seconds count. As a recruiter, this is what I'm paying attention to. - Keep your résumé clean, organized, and easy to read. No fancy templates. Clear headings. Consistent font. - Quantify your work. “Increased revenue by 15%” says more than “responsible for improving sales.” - Add context. If you’ve had short stints, include a short reason, contract ended, company restructured, relocation, etc. - At a glance, multiple short roles can look like “job-hopping,” even when that’s not true. A single line of context helps a recruiter understand your story instead of making assumptions. - Know the difference between soft skills and hard skills. Recruiters care about proven, measurable skills. “Team player” is an opinion. “Proficient in Python” or “experienced with HubSpot CRM” is evidence. - Show impact. Replace job duties with results , what changed because you were there? - Be transparent. Be clear about what you’re looking for next, salary expectations, or what kind of environment you thrive in. A good recruiter will use that information to advocate for you. Because yes, AI can rank résumés, but it can’t recognize potential. I’d love to hear from other recruiters who still take the time, what makes a candidate stand out to you, not the system?

    30 1 Comment

  • David NGARAMBE
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Your recruitment agency's top performer places 3x more than everyone else. And you have no idea how she does it. The Problem Sarah closes 15 placements a month. The rest of your team closes 5. You ask Sarah what she does differently. She shrugs: "I just... do my thing." Her success lives in her head: - The questions she asks in screening calls - How she pitches roles to passive candidates - When she follows up and what she says - How she handles objections - Which clients she prioritizes Your other recruiters can't learn from what they can't see. Sarah's methods stay with Sarah. When she's on vacation, placements drop. When she eventually leaves, her magic leaves too. You're paying for 10 recruiters but only getting the output of 3. What Most Agencies Do (Probably You Do Too): They tell top performers to "train the team." Sarah runs a workshop. Shares some tips. Nothing changes. Because learning happens in the real moment, not in theory. They create playbooks no one follows. They do ride-alongs that capture 1% of what matters. They hope new hires "figure it out." Some do. Most don't. Performance stays wildly inconsistent. The gap between your best and worst gets bigger. My Recommendation: Implement an AI system that captures everything Sarah does. It watches her patterns: - The exact questions that get candidates excited - The follow-up timing that closes deals - The messaging that gets responses - The objection-handling that wins Then AI guides other recruiters in real-time: - "Top performers ask about career goals at this point" - "Sarah follows up 24 hours after this stage" - "High-converting messages use this structure" Your average recruiter gets Sarah's playbook as they work. Not in training. In the actual calls and emails. Performance across the team levels up. Sarah's 15 placements becomes everyone's baseline. The multiplier: Capture your best. Scale it to everyone.

    Like Comment

  • Brandon Stevens
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    AI is here to help your job search! Or is it? Let's be blunt: No, it's not. That new AI tool promising to apply to 100 jobs for you isn't a magic wand; it's a megaphone in a stadium where everyone is already shouting. AI, as it's being used by most platforms, isn't fixing the problem. It's just accelerating a broken system and making the noise unbearable for both job seekers and employers. As someone who has built talent intelligence systems for years, I'm deeply concerned. The "easy apply" model has destroyed the hiring experience, and AI is its new super-weapon. Here's the hard truth: In this case, efficiency is hurting your job search. Applying for a high-level job isn't a transactional B2C purchase. It should be a relationship-driven B2B sale. You are the expert, and you are making a high-stakes, consultative sale to a specific client (the company). But the "easy apply" model forces you to act like a low-value commodity. I've heard job seekers say they "don't have time" to slow down and focus on fewer employers. That's how entrenched this broken model has become. Inside corporate HR teams, it's just as bad. The system encourages gatekeepers to function like basic admins, not talent analysts. Quantity over quality fails everyone. How to Win Now: Think Like a Marketer, Not an Applicant You can adopt this strategy today. Stop being a "job applicant" and start being your own high-level, well-connected recruiter. 1. Stop the Volume Game. Ditch the 100-app-a-day myth. It's a waste of your time and emotional energy. 2. Build a Target List. Adopt an "account-based marketing" model. Identify 10-15 companies you actually want to work for. 3. Find the Problem, Not the Posting. Use your network to find people at those companies. Don't ask for a job. Ask what problems their team is facing. 4. Deliver the Solution. Use that "back-door" approach. Send a specific, detailed inquiry showing exactly how your skills (optimism, empathy, resiliency, and focus, plus your hard skills) can help them solve that specific problem. Stop being a number in the stack. Be the solution they've been looking for. I provide more detail and context in this Medium blog post.

    Why AI Is Accelerating a Broken Job Market (And How to Actually Get Hired) medium.com
    10
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Dr. Bijun L.
    8mo
    • Report this post

    𝗦𝗼𝗵𝗻𝘂𝘀 𝗧𝗲𝗰𝗵 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝘂𝗺𝗻 |  𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝘂𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗥𝗲𝗰𝗿𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴: 𝗡𝗼𝘁 "𝗣𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗝𝗼𝗯𝘀," 𝗯𝘂𝘁 "𝗝𝗼𝗯𝘀 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗣𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲" We face a profound paradox in today's talent market: companies urgently need skilled professionals, yet a vast pool of qualified talent is locked out by the very digital tools meant to help them. Where is the disconnect? I believe the problem lies in a fundamental model. For decades, our digital recruitment tools are built around "search". This model operates on an implicit assumption: 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗷𝗼𝗯 𝘀𝗲𝗲𝗸𝗲𝗿 𝗺𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗮𝗹𝘀𝗼 𝗯𝗲 𝗮𝗻 𝗲𝘅𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘁 "𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿." 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗢𝗹𝗱 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗶𝗴𝗺 ("𝗣𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗼"): 𝗔 𝗗𝗶𝗴𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆 The current model functions like a massive digital directory. Platforms display the "items" (jobs) on their shelves and expect the user (the job seeker) to navigate the aisles, knowing the exact keywords and filters to find what they need. This model demands that the user:  1. 𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝗦𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵: They must constantly browse and filter.  2. 𝗠𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗿 "𝗞𝗲𝘆𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗱𝘀": They must know how to package their skills and experience into an "algorithm-friendly" resume.  3. 𝗢𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗙𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: They must tolerate complex filters, tedious forms, and a high rate of failure. For the skilled, hands-on workforce that forms the backbone of our society, this barrier is disproportionately high. Their skills are practical, not "keyword-optimized." They need results, not a search process. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗣𝗮𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗶𝗴𝗺 ("𝗜𝗻𝗳𝗼 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗱𝘀 𝗣𝗲𝗼𝗽𝗹𝗲"): 𝗔𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗲𝗿𝗴𝗲 A profound paradigm shift is underway—moving from "search" to "intelligent distribution." This new model completely inverts the relationship between information and people. It's not a directory but an intelligent concierge. In this model, the system no longer waits passively to be searched, it 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗹𝘆 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻. The user doesn't need a perfect CV, but simply describe their experience and needs in the most natural way (perhaps in a simple conversation). The technology powering this is fundamentally different:  1. 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 "𝗞𝗲𝘆𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗱 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴" 𝘁𝗼 "𝗗𝗲𝗲𝗽 𝗨𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴": The AI's core task is no longer to match strings of text, but to truly understand the real skills and potential behind unstructured data (like spoken language, dialects, or simple descriptions).  2. 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 "𝗣𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲" 𝘁𝗼 "𝗔𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗯𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲": The system's value is no longer measured by how "big" it is, but by how "precise" it is. It uses AI to predict fit and then actively and precisely "pushes" the best-matched opportunities to the right people. #HRTech #AI #FutureOfWork #UserExperience #IntelligentDistribution #Hiring #SkillsGap #SohnusTechColumn

  • Zain Tanveer
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Recently, one of our recruiting agency clients reached out with a familiar challenge: they needed to scale their operations without sacrificing quality or burning out their team. Over the past few months, I've been working closely with them to build and optimize systems that have transformed how they operate. Here's what we implemented: Business Development System: Automated their outreach and follow-up processes, allowing them to engage more prospects consistently while their team focuses on building relationships. MPC Campaigns: Designed campaigns to strategically market their strongest candidates to the right clients, increasing placement rates and showcasing top talent proactively. Candidate Sourcing: Built automated workflows to identify, engage, and nurture top talent, dramatically reducing time-to-fill while improving candidate quality. These aren't "set it and forget it" solutions. I've been working alongside their team on recurring execution, maintenance, and continuous improvement, ensuring the systems evolve with their business needs. The result: they're scaling efficiently, they are more productive, and are delivering better results for their clients. Are you in recruiting and feeling the pressure to scale? I'd love to discuss how automation can help you grow without the growing pains.

  • Sean Quigley
    8mo
    • Report this post

    It’s hard to pick favorites… But here are the 10 real-world ways we use AI at Advantage.ai — ranked by the biggest value impact for our customers 👇 1️⃣ AI Recruiting Assistant – Finds, matches, and texts candidates on your behalf. Conversationally collects prescreens, confirms interest, submits qualified candidates to your ATS — and even answers the phone when candidates call back. 2️⃣ Voice Agents – Handle prescreens and candidate questions 24/7 by phone, with the option to warm-transfer qualified calls to recruiters during business hours. With multiple voice styles to choose from, it’s a perfect fit for tracked phone numbers on ads and landing pages. 3️⃣ Chat-to-Apply – Turns text conversations into completed applications in seconds. Answers candidate questions automatically, easy to set up, and can even go live the same day. 4️⃣ AI + Automation for Media Buying – Continuously optimizes job ads across search, social, and job boards for quality, performance, and cost. It’s taken a complex mix of tech to reach this “ideal state” but we've made it. 5️⃣ Candidate Scoring & Matching – Ranks talent by fit and automatically re-matches candidates in your CRM when new jobs open — re-engaging great people who might have gone quiet. 6️⃣ AI-Enhanced Career Sites – Included with your ad buys: branded, searchable, domain-hosted career sites with built-in voice and chat agents that engage and convert visitors from every source. 7️⃣ Smart Qualifying Questions – Auto-creates short prescreen questions from job data, ensuring only qualified candidates make it through to full ATS applications. 8️⃣ AI Job Writer – Writes and improves job descriptions to attract the right talent. Sure, you can just do this in ChatGPT — but it’s a lot better when it’s built into your workflow as a one-click option. 9️⃣ AI Data Insights – Explains campaign performance in plain English, builds charts, and helps users navigate analytics tools. This one becomes a novelty once you learn the reporting UI and embedded charts, but it's still cool. 🔟 Universal Easy Apply – Makes it seamless for candidates to apply on Indeed, Google, Facebook, ZipRecruiter, Talroo, Jobcase, JobGet, and more. (Okay, this one’s not pure AI — but it deserves a spot because you do get more qualified candidates per dollar spent) If you’re in #TalentAcquisition and want to demo any of this, just reach out. Or tell me — what’s one AI use case you wish your recruiting, sourcing, or recruitment marketing team had today? #RecruitmentMarketing #AIinRecruiting #HiringAutomation #HRTech #FutureOfHiring #ProgrammaticAdvertising #TalentAcquisition

  • Buzzli

    966 followers

    7mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Recruiters and prospects do not read your headline. They search it. If it does not match their queries, you stay invisible. Reverse engineer your headline for LinkedIn search. Use this 5-part, 220 character build: Role | ICP | Outcome | Proof | Priority keywords Example: Product Marketing Manager | B2B SaaS | Launches that double pipeline | Ex Google | PLG, positioning, pricing, GTM How to find the right words: - Scan 10 target job posts. Capture title variants, must-have skills, tools. - Type your role in LinkedIn search. Note autocomplete and People also search for. - Review 5 top profiles. Copy recurring skills and phrasing. - Reflect recruiter filters you know. Location, industry, seniority. Test it with a simple 14-day A/B: - Baseline today. Search appearances, profile views, and followers. - Week 1: Headline A. - Week 2: Headline B. Keep your activity similar. - Pick the winner. Iterate monthly. AI can do this for you in seconds with Buzzli's AI powered LinkedIn Profile Analyzer: https://lnkd.in/g3cEmKnE #LinkedInProfile #LinkedInTips #PersonalBranding
